coredata.dc:title: pH-mediated reversible fluorescence nanoswitch based on inner filter effect induced fluorescence quenching for selective and visual detection of 4-nitrophenol
coredata.dc:creator: Han, Lei
Liu, Shi Gang
Liang, Jia Yu
Ju, Yan Jun
Li, Nian Bing
Luo, Hong Qun
coredata.dc:description: Being a common hazardous waste, 4-nitrophenol (4-NP) has caused a serious threat to humans and environment. Therefore, rapid and selective detection of 4-NP, especially using a simple and portable instrument, is highly desired for human health and environmental monitoring. Herein, we develop a novel pH-mediated reversible fluorescence nanoswitch for selectively detecting 4-NP by using water-soluble fluorescent polymer carbon dots (PCDs) as a probe. The fluorescence of PCDs can be quenched by 4-NP via inner filter effect (IFE) because its excitation spectrum well overlaps with the absorption spectrum of 4-NP under alkaline condition. However, an obvious blue shift of the absorption peak of 4-NP occurs under acidic condition, causing the fluorescence recovery of PCDs due to the disappearance of IFE. On the basis of this principle, a pH-mediated reversible fluorescence nanoswitch was constructed and a broad linear range was obtained from 0.5 to 60 μM with a detection limit of 0.26 μM for 4-NP. Furthermore, this approach was successfully applied to detect 4-NP in real water samples and a portable polyamide film-based sensor was developed for visual detection of 4-NP, which offers a promising platform for the detection of 4-NP in on-site and resource-poor settings.
coredata.dcterms:subject: 4-nitrophenol
Polymer carbon dots
pH-mediated fluorescence nanoswitch
Inner filter effect
Visual detection

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Fabrication of fluorescent PCDs impregnated polyamide film
Detection of 4-NP in real water samples
Preparation and characterization of fluorescent PCDs
Fluorescence sensing of 4-NP based on the fluorescent PCDs probe
Mechanism of fluorescence response of the PCDs toward 4-NP
Application of the proposed method in drinking and environmental water samples
Visual detection of 4-NP based on polyamide film sensor

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Schematic illustration of the synthetic process of the fluorescent PCDs (A) and the principle of pH-mediated reversible fluorescence nanoswitch for selective detection of 4-NP based on IFE (B).

(A) Fluorescence and UV–vis absorption spectra of PCDs. Insets are the photographs of the PCDs solution under 365 nm UV light (left) and visible light (right). Deconvoluted high-resolution XPS spectra of PCDs for (B) C1s, (C) N1s, and (D) O1s.

(A) Fluorescence spectra of PCDs after addition of various concentrations of 4-NP. Inset exhibits the photographs of fluorescent PCDs solution before (left) and after (right) the addition of 4-NP under 365 nm UV light. (B) The relationship between the fluorescence quenching efficiency ((F0-F)/F0) and the concentration of 4-NP. The inset exhibits the linear relationship between the ((F0-F)/F0) and the concentration of 4-NP. (C) Fluorescence quenching efficiency of aromatic compounds (50 μM) toward PCDs. (D) Fluorescence quenching efficiency of common inorganic ions (50 μM) and 4-NP (50 μM) toward PCDs. All experiments were conducted in pH 8.0 BR buffer.

(A) UV–vis absorption spectra of the PCDs (a) and 4-NP (b) as well as the experimental (c) and theoretical (d) absorption spectra of the mixture of PCDs and 4-NP in pH 8.0 BR buffer. (B) UV–vis absorption spectrum of 4-NP (50 μM) in pH 8.0 BR buffer (a), fluorescence excitation spectrum (b), and emission spectrum (c) of PCDs. (C) The effect of temperature on the fluorescence quenching efficiency. (D) UV–vis absorption spectra of 2-NP (50 μM), 3-NP (50 μM), and 4-NP (50 μM) in pH 8.0 BR buffer and fluorescence excitation spectrum of PCDs.

(A) UV–vis absorption spectra and (B) the corresponding photograph of 4-NP (50 μM) in different pH BR buffers. (C) UV–vis absorption spectra of 4-NP (50 μM) in different pH BR buffers (a:5.0, b:6.0, c:7.0, and d:8.0) and fluorescence excitation spectrum of PCDs (e). (D) Fluorescence intensity of PCDs in different pH BR buffer before and after the addition of 50 μM 4-NP. (E) UV–vis absorption spectra of the nanoswitch in different pH solutions (a: pH 6, b: pH 8) and the emission spectrum of PCDs (c). (F) Reversibility of the as-developed fluorescence nanoswitch mediated by pH. Inset exhibits the corresponding photographs of the solution in different pH conditions. The pH of solution was mediated by alternately adding 0.02 M NaOH and 0.02 M HCl to the mixture of PCDs and 4-NP (50 μM).

(A) Photographs of the as-prepared polyamide film immobilized fluorescent PCDs under 365 nm UV light. (B) Photographs of the as-prepared polyamide film after addition of various concentrations of 4-NP (from left to right by adding 5 μL of 0.005 0.01, 0.05, 0.1, 0.5, 1, 5, and 10 mM 4-NP). (C) Photographs of the as-prepared polyamide film after addition of 5 μL of 5 mM 4-NP and its analogs (from left to right: PG, HQ, 4-NT, 4-CP, AN, 3-NP, 2-NP, and 4-NP).
Analytical results of 4-NP in real water samples by our proposed method (n = 3).

head.ce:title: pH-mediated reversible fluorescence nanoswitch based on inner filter effect induced fluorescence quenching for selective and visual detection of 4-nitrophenol

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Key Laboratory of Eco-environments in Three Gorges Reservoir Region (Ministry of Education), School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Southwest University, Chongqing 400715, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Key Laboratory of Eco-environments in Three Gorges Reservoir Region (Ministry of Education)
School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Corresponding authors at: Tiansheng Road, BeiBei District, Chongqing, 400715, PR China.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Being a common hazardous waste, 4-nitrophenol (4-NP) has caused a serious threat to humans and environment. Therefore, rapid and selective detection of 4-NP, especially using a simple and portable instrument, is highly desired for human health and environmental monitoring. Herein, we develop a novel pH-mediated reversible fluorescence nanoswitch for selectively detecting 4-NP by using water-soluble fluorescent polymer carbon dots (PCDs) as a probe. The fluorescence of PCDs can be quenched by 4-NP via inner filter effect (IFE) because its excitation spectrum well overlaps with the absorption spectrum of 4-NP under alkaline condition. However, an obvious blue shift of the absorption peak of 4-NP occurs under acidic condition, causing the fluorescence recovery of PCDs due to the disappearance of IFE. On the basis of this principle, a pH-mediated reversible fluorescence nanoswitch was constructed and a broad linear range was obtained from 0.5 to 60 μM with a detection limit of 0.26 μM for 4-NP. Furthermore, this approach was successfully applied to detect 4-NP in real water samples and a portable polyamide film-based sensor was developed for visual detection of 4-NP, which offers a promising platform for the detection of 4-NP in on-site and resource-poor settings.

ce:section.ce:para: 4-Nitrophenol (4-NP), a type of common and significant nitro-aromatic compound, has been widely utilized in the pesticides, explosives, dyes, and pharmaceutical industries [1,2]. However, 4-NP is also a kind of refractory environmental pollutant and deleterious waste. It has been reported that 4-NP can cause a serious threat to human health, such as methemoglobinemia, headaches, and the injuries to the liver, kidney, and central nervous system [3–5]. Considering its highly toxic effects, 4-NP has been listed as a toxic priority pollutant and its maximum allowable level in drinking water has been established by the United States Environmental Protection Agency (USEPA) is 60 μg/L (∼0.43 μM) [6,7]. Furthermore, it is extremely difficult to remove 4-NP from the polluted wastewater and soil due to its high stability and poor biodegradability [8]. Accordingly, it is highly desirable to develop a simple, rapid, and efficient method for selectively detecting and monitoring the level of 4-NP in drinking and environmental water samples. Currently, a number of strategies have been designed to detect 4-NP, including liquid chromatography-tandem mass spectrometry [9], electrochemistry [10–12], photoelectrochemistry [13], high-performance liquid chromatography [14], and fuel cell-based sensing platform [15,16]. Unfortunately, these methods generally require bulky and expensive instruments, complicated sample pretreatment or electrode modifications, time-consuming detection process, and low distinguishing ability toward 4-NP, 2-nitrophenol (2-NP), and 3-nitrophenol (3-NP), which limit their broad applications.

Fluorescence spectroscopy as a powerful analytical technique with high sensitivity and selectivity, quick responsiveness, convenience, and real-time analysis has been extensively used in the field of analytical chemistry [17,18]. Recently, some interesting fluorescence sensing platforms have been developed for the detection of 4-NP [4,19–28]. However, most of these fluorescent sensing platforms are based on the electron transfer (ET) [19–25] or Förster resonance energy transfer (FRET) [4,26,27] induced fluorescence quenching. In order to achieve the sensitive detection of 4-NP, the surface modification and chemical conjugation are generally required between the donor (fluorophore) and acceptor (4-NP) because the quenching efficiency based on ET or FRET induced fluorescence quenching is dependent on the distance between donor and acceptor [29,30]. Moreover, the 2-NP and 3-NP would interfere with the detection of 4-NP due to their analogous chemical structures. To achieve the selective detection of 4-NP, the molecular imprinting technique is introduced, which will suffer from a tedious process such as the introduction and remove of template or surface modification [4,19,21,25]. In contrast, inner filter effect (IFE)-based induced fluorescence quenching can provide more simplicity and flexibility in the construction of sensors because it does not require any surface modification and covalent linking between absorber and fluorophore [31,32]. Nevertheless, the design and development of IFE-based fluorescent sensor are still challenging due to the restricted selection of appropriate absorber and fluorophore, since IFE can occur effectively only if the absorption spectrum of the absorber well overlaps with the excitation or emission spectrum of the fluorophore [33,34]. Therefore, it is still highly desirable and challenging to construct IFE-based fluorescence sensing platform for sensitive and selective detection of 4-NP.

Fluorescent polymer carbon dots (PCDs), as a specific type of carbon dots, are generally prepared from the small molecules or polymer by dehydration, condensation, and carbonization [35,36]. Fluorescent PCDs have triggered tremendous attention in recent years for appealing application potentials in drug delivery and therapy, bioimaging, and sensing due to its low toxicity, good photostability, low-cost of fabrication, and excellent optical property [37,38]. Although the fluorescent PCDs have achieved potential applications in these fields, it is still very meaningful and interesting to further broaden the appealing applications of fluorescent PCDs.

In the present work, we design a novel pH-mediated reversible fluorescence nanoswitch for selective and visual detection of 4-NP based on IFE. As illustrated in Scheme 1 , the green fluorescent PCDs are synthesized and used as a fluorescent probe to detect 4-NP. The fluorescence of PCDs can be quenched by 4-NP via IFE because its excitation spectrum well overlaps with the absorption spectrum of 4-NP under alkaline condition. However, an obvious blue shift of the absorption peak of 4-NP occurs under acidic condition, which makes its absorption spectrum scarcely overlap with the excitation spectrum of PCDs and causes the fluorescence recovery of PCDs due to the disappearance of IFE. Based on this principle, a pH-mediated reversible fluorescence nanoswitch was constructed for sensitive and selective detection of 4-NP. The proposed method was also successfully applied to detect 4-NP in real water samples and a portable polyamide film-based sensor was developed for visual detection of 4-NP. In addition, we investigated and discussed the 4-NP-induced fluorescence quenching mechanism in detail, confirming that the primary fluorescence quenching mechanism was IFE.

Hydroquinone (HQ), phloroglucinol (PG), 4-nitrotoluene (4-NT), 4-chlorophenol (4-CP), p-phenylenediamine (p-PD), 2-NP, 3-NP, and 4-NP were bought from Aladdin Ltd. (Shanghai, China). Ethylenediamine (EDA), sodium hydroxide (NaOH), hydrochloric acid (HCl), petroleum ether, ethyl acetate, aniline (AN), methylbenzene (PhMe), and all of the inorganic salts were acquired from Chengdu Kelong Chemical Reagents Factory (Sichuan, China). All chemicals were analytical reagent grade. Ultrapure water (18.2 MΩ cm) was utilized in all aqueous solutions.

Fluorescence and UV–vis absorption spectra were recorded by using an F-2700 spectrofluorometer (Hitachi, Japan) and UV–vis 2450 spectrophotometer (Shimadzu, Japan), respectively. Fourier transform infrared (FT-IR) spectrum was obtained using a Bruker IFS 113v spectrometer (Bruker, Germany). X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) analyses were performed using an ESCALAB 250Xi photoelectron spectrometer (Thermo Scientific, USA). The time-resolved fluorescence decay measurements were carried out with an Edinburgh FL 920 fluorescence spectrometer (Edinburgh, U.K.). Transmission electron microscope (TEM) image was obtained from a Tecnai G2 F20 field emission transmission electron microscopy (FEI, USA). The zeta potentials were determined by using a Nano Brook Omni zeta potentiometric analyzer (Brookhaven, USA). The pH measurements were carried out with an FE28 digital pH meter (Mettler-Toledo, Switzerland).

The fluorescent PCDs were prepared according to our previously reported method [39]. In brief, 1 mL of 1 M ethylenediamine was added to 20 mL of 0.1 M hydroquinone solution. The resulting solution was mixed and heated in a water bath at 50 °C for 2 h. Subsequently, the fluorescent PCDs were purified by silica column chromatography using ethyl acetate and petroleum ether as eluents. The purified fluorescent PCDs were dispersed in ultrapure water and used for subsequent experiments.

As for the detection of 4-NP, 10 μL of stock solutions of fluorescent PCDs were added to a 1.5 mL of plastic centrifuge tube containing 970 μL of pH 8.0 Britton-Robinson (BR) buffer solutions. Subsequently, 4-NP solutions with various concentrations were added to the above solution. The total volume of mixtures was 1.0 mL. The mixtures were adequately blended and incubated for 1 min. The fluorescence spectra of the mixture were then recorded under excitation wavelength at 410 nm. The selectivity of fluorescent PCDs toward 4-NP was estimated by adding other potential interference substances instead of 4-NP in a similar way as described above. For pH-mediated reversible measurements, the pH of the solution was mediated by alternately adding 0.02 M NaOH and 0.02 M HCl.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fabrication of fluorescent PCDs impregnated polyamide film
ce:section.ce:para: Polyamide film-based sensor was fabricated by immobilization of the fluorescent PCDs on polyamide film. Prior to fabrication, the stock solution of fluorescent PCDs was diluted 20-fold with pH 8.0 BR buffer. Subsequently, the large polyamide film (5 × 10 cm) was cut into small circular polyamide film with the same diameter by using a 6 mm stopper borer and then the small circular polyamide film was immersed into the above solution for 3 min. Finally, the circular polyamide film was taken out and dried at room temperature for subsequent visual detection of 4-NP.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Detection of 4-NP in real water samples
ce:section.ce:para: The tap water sample was obtained from our laboratory and river water sample was collected from the Yangtse River (Chongqing, China). The tap water was directly used and river water was filtered by a 0.2 μm filtration membrane to remove suspended particles, and then the filtrate was treated by centrifugation at 10,000 rpm for 10 min. Subsequently, the pretreated water samples were spiked with various concentrations of 4-NP (0, 5, 10, and 20 μM) and the fluorescence spectra of spiked samples were collected by the method described above.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation and characterization of fluorescent PCDs
ce:section.ce:para: The fluorescent PCDs were prepared according to our previous report [39]. As depicted in Fig. 1 A, the as-obtained fluorescent PCDs show strong green fluorescence and excellent aqueous solubility with the maximum excitation and emission wavelengths at 410 and 510 nm, respectively. The UV–vis absorption spectrum of fluorescent PCDs presents two characteristic absorption peaks at 234 and 410 nm, which can be ascribed to the π–π* transition of CC and n–π* transition of CN, respectively [40]. The TEM image suggests the successful synthesis of fluorescent PCDs (Fig. S1 A). The FT-IR spectrum reveals that the synthesized fluorescent PCDs mainly include –NH2 (3235 and 3044 cm−1), −CH2– (2959 and 2854 cm−1), CN (1630 cm−1), CN (1516 cm−1), CN (1345 cm−1), CNH (1226 cm−1), CO (1084 cm−1), and aromatic CC (1578, 1516, and 1459 cm−1) (Fig. S1B) [41–44]. As displayed in Fig. 1B–D, the XPS analysis also confirms the results of FT-IR analysis. The deconvoluted C1s spectrum of PCDs reveals the existence of CC (284.6 eV), CC (283.5 eV), CN (287.6 eV), and CN/CO (285.5 eV) [45]. The fitted N1s spectrum suggests the presence of CNC (400.5 eV), NH2 (399.1 eV), and CN (398.5 eV) [46]. The high-resolution O1s spectrum verifies the presence of COC/COH (532.1 eV) [47]. The results of FT-IR and XPS analysis indicate that the synthesized fluorescent PCDs possess abundant hydrophilic groups, such as –NH2 and CO, which endow them excellent aqueous solubility.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fluorescence sensing of 4-NP based on the fluorescent PCDs probe
ce:section.ce:para: Considering that the PCDs possess excellent aqueous solubility, strong fluorescence, and unique optical properties, especially the excitation spectrum completely overlaps with the absorption spectrum of 4-NP under alkaline condition, the PCDs were utilized as a fluorescent probe for rapid and selective detection of 4-NP. The experimental conditions were optimized to obtain a superior sensing performance. Firstly, the influence of pH on the detection of 4-NP was investigated. As shown in Fig. S2, the fluorescence quenching efficiency ((F-F0)/F0) of 4-NP toward PCDs is highly dependent on the pH values of the solution, where F0 and F represent the fluorescence intensity of sensing system at the emission wavelength of 510 nm in the absence and presence of 4-NP, respectively. The quenching efficiency increases progressively as the increase of pH values and arrives at a maximum when the pH of the sensing system is up to 8.0. Thus, the subsequent experiments were performed in pH 8.0 BR buffer. Subsequently, the response time was studied. As depicted in Fig. S3, the fluorescence intensity of PCDs decreases immediately and keeps constant after 1 min, revealing that the detection procedure can be rapidly completed. It is highly desirable for the on-site and practical detection.

Under the above optimal conditions, the fluorescent sensing platform was constructed by adding variable concentrations of 4-NP to the solution of fluorescent PCDs. As presented in Fig. 2 A, the fluorescence intensity of the PCDs declines gradually as the increase of 4-NP concentrations ranging from 0 to 100 μM. Fig. 2B depicts the relationship between the fluorescence quenching efficiency and the concentration of 4-NP, and a good linear relationship (R 2 = 0.9908) can be obtained over the range from 0.5 to 60 μM (Inset of Fig. 2B). The fitted linear equation can be described as y = 0.01711x + 0.03051 (where y denotes the fluorescence quenching efficiency and x represents the concentration of 4-NP). The limit of detection (LOD) was determined to be as low as 0.26 μM based on 3σ/k (where σ is the standard deviation of blank solution and k is the slope of the calibration curve), which is lower than the maximum allowable content of 4-NP in drinking water (∼0.43 μM) guided by USEPA. The performances (such as detection limit, accuracy, detection time, and linear range) of our developed method are able to comparable to some previously reported methods for 4-NP detection (Table S1). More significantly, the entire detection process for 4-NP can be completed rapidly with extremely simple operation process and does not need sophisticated pretreatment procedures, indicating that this method possesses favorable potentiality for the detection of 4-NP in practical samples.

Subsequently, selectivity investigations were performed to further assess the performance of the as-developed sensing platform. Some aromatic compounds with a similar chemical structure including 2-NP, 3-NP, HQ, PG, 4-NT, AN, PhMe, 4-CP, and p-PD were selected as potential interference substances to evaluate the selectivity. As illustrated in Fig. 2C, only 4-NP induces an obvious fluorescence quenching of PCDs, whereas other aromatic compounds only cause an ignorable fluorescence change of PCDs. The result suggests that our proposed method exhibits a good selectivity toward 4-NP. More significantly, it can selectively identify 4-NP from 2-NP and 3-NP. In addition, some of the common inorganic ions including K+, Na+, Ca2+, Mg2+, Cu2+, Al3+, Fe3+, Hg2+, Pb2+, Ag+, Cd2+, Co2+, Ni2+, Zn2+, Cr3+, F−, Cl−, CO3 2-, SO4 2-, and NO3 − that may exist in real water samples were also studied. As depicted in Fig. 2D, these common inorganic ions have no remarkable influence on the detection of 4-NP. These results further indicate that our proposed method has a good selectivity toward 4-NP and can be used to detect selectively 4-NP in the practical water samples.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Mechanism of fluorescence response of the PCDs toward 4-NP
ce:section.ce:para: It is well known that 4-NP is an electron-deficient compound due to the strong electron withdrawing effect of nitro-group, which makes it become a kind of good electron-acceptor substrate. Therefore, a series of sensors have been designed to detect 4-NP via ET [19–25]. In this study, 4-NP induced an obvious fluorescence quenching of the fluorescent PCDs. However, other similar electron-deficient nitro-aromatic compounds such as 2-NP and 3-NP caused a negligible fluorescence quenching of PCDs, which indicates that ET is not the primary quenching mechanism. In order to investigate and prove the quenching mechanism between fluorescent PCDs and 4-NP, a series of experiments were carried out. The UV–vis absorption spectra of fluorescent PCDs, 4-NP, and the mixtures of fluorescent PCDs and 4-NP were measured. As displayed in Fig. 3 A, the experimental UV–vis absorption spectrum of PCDs in the presence of 4-NP completely overlaps with the theoretical one. This result indicates that the interaction between PCDs and 4-NP is extremely weak, and does not form complexes between them [48,49]. Moreover, the zeta potential of fluorescent PCDs and the 4-NP in pH 8.0 BR buffer is −21.34 and −24.73 mV, respectively, revealing that there is electrostatic repulsion between the fluorescent PCDs and 4-NP (Fig. S4). Therefore, ET is not the dominating quenching mechanism in this sensor.

Subsequently, the excitation and emission spectra of fluorescent PCDs were investigated. As shown in Fig. 3B, there is no overlap between the UV–vis absorption spectrum of 4-NP and fluorescence emission spectrum of the PCDs, which suggests that the FRET does not exist in this fluorescence quenching progress. Since the occurrence of FRET requires that the emission spectrum of the fluorophore (donor) has a large overlap with the absorption spectrum of the quencher (acceptor) [50]. Moreover, the efficiency of FRET is also dependent on the distance between the fluorescence donor and acceptor. In general, the distance between fluorescence donor and acceptor should be less than 10 nm for FRET [51].

In contrast, the fluorescence excitation spectrum of PCDs almost completely overlaps with the absorption spectrum of 4-NP in pH 8.0 BR buffer (Fig. 3B). Therefore, IFE was considered to be the predominant quenching mechanism in this fluorescence quenching process. To demonstrate this hypothesis, the corresponding fluorescence lifetimes were measured. Fig. S5 shows the time-resolved fluorescence spectra of PCDs before and after the addition of 4-NP, respectively. It can be seen that fluorescence lifetimes change very little after the addition of 50 μM 4-NP, which indicates that the 4-NP-induced fluorescence quenching of PCDs mainly derives from IFE [52]. Moreover, the temperature has little influence on fluorescence quenching efficiency, further demonstrating that the IFE is the main quenching mechanism (Fig. 3C) [53]. In addition, this sensor can selectively identify 4-NP from 2-NP and 3-NP, which is because the UV–vis absorption spectra of same concentration 2-NP and 3-NP have little overlap with the excitation spectrum of PCDs (Fig. 3D). The result also confirms that the 4-NP-induced fluorescence quenching of PCDs derives from IFE.

Finally, the pH-dependent fluorescence quenching efficiency of 4-NP toward PCDs further demonstrated that the IFE was the primary quenching mechanism. As displayed in Fig. 4 A and B, with the increase of pH an obvious bathochromic shift of the absorption peak of 4-NP occurs ranging from approximately 320 to 400 nm and a remarkable color change from colorless to yellow can be observed by the naked eye. The bathochromic shift of absorption peak of 4-NP is attributed to the formation of oxygen anion of 4-NP under the alkaline condition. Since the strong electron-withdrawing effect of nitro-group causes the dissociation of H+ from hydroxyl, making the 4-NP become a weak acid with a pKa 7.12 [26]. Under acidic conditions, the 4-NP exists in the form of molecule. However, OH− can capture the H+ from hydroxyl of 4-NP to form the oxygen anion of 4-NP under alkaline condition (Scheme 1) [54,55]. The formation of oxygen anion of 4-NP causes the increase of conjugated degree and leads to the bathochromic shift of absorption peak of 4-NP. As shown in Fig. 4C, there is almost no overlap between the UV–vis absorption spectrum of 4-NP and fluorescence excitation spectrum of the PCDs when the pH of the sensing system is less than 6.0, and the fluorescence intensity of PCDs does not change obviously before and after the addition of 4-NP (Fig. 4D). With increasing pH of the sensing system, a remarkable fluorescence quenching is observed and the difference of fluorescence intensity (F0-F) arrives at a maximum when the pH of the sensing system is up to 8.0 (Fig. 4D). This is because the bathochromic shift of absorption peak of 4-NP with the increase of pH causes the increase of the overlap degree between the absorption spectrum of 4-NP and the fluorescence excitation spectrum of PCDs. These results further reveal that the 4-NP-induced fluorescence quenching of PCDs derives from IFE.

Since 4-NP is a kind of weak acid, the dissociation is reversible. Fig. 4E displays the absorption spectrum of 4-NP with a maximum absorption peak at around 400 nm in pH 8 aqueous solution mediated by NaOH, which well overlaps with the excitation spectrum of PCDs, leading to the fluorescence quenching. However, the maximum absorption peak of 4-NP blueshift occurs to approximately 320 nm in pH 6 aqueous solution mediated by HCl, which almost does not overlap with the excitation spectrum of PCDs, causing the fluorescence recovery due to the disappearance of IFE. Based on this phenomenon, a pH-mediated reversible fluorescence nanoswitch can be constructed to detect 4-NP. Moreover, this fluorescence nanoswitch shows an excellent reversibility that the fluorescence of PCDs is reversibly regulated by pH more than three consecutive cycles without remarkable fatigue (Fig. 4F).

Based on the above discussion, it can be concluded that our developed fluorescence nanoswitch for the selective detection of 4-NP is based on the IFE-induced the fluorescence quenching, which is different from reported fluorescence methods for the detection of 4-NP that are generally based on the fluorescence quenching induced by ET or FRET. To the best of our knowledge, it is the first time of using IFE-based pH-mediated reversible fluorescence sensing platform for the detection of 4-NP.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Application of the proposed method in drinking and environmental water samples
ce:section.ce:para: To evaluate the feasibility of our proposed method, the detection of 4-NP was performed in real samples, such as tap water and Yangtse River water. The results suggest that no 4-NP exists in the tested water samples (Table 1 ). Thus, the standard addition experiments were performed by spiked with various concentrations of standard 4-NP solutions (5, 10, and 20 μM) into the tap and Yangtse River water samples. As shown in Table 1, the values measured by our proposed method are consistent with the spiked values for 4-NP concentration. The recoveries of 4-NP in the real water samples range from 96.80% to 103.80% with the relative standard deviation (RSD) between 1.85% and 4.07%, suggesting good accuracy and precision of the proposed method.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Visual detection of 4-NP based on polyamide film sensor
ce:section.ce:para: Considering the quick response, simplicity, and free-label of this method, a portable polyamide film-based sensor was developed for visual detection of 4-NP. As displayed in Fig. 5 A, the fluorescent PCDs probe is successfully immobilized onto polyamide film and exhibits strong fluorescence under 365 nm UV light. However, the fluorescence quenches immediately after adding 5 μL of various concentrations of 4-NP solutions (0.005 0.01, 0.05, 0.1, 0.5, 1, 5 and 10 mM) and the related change can be observed by the naked eye under 365 nm UV light (Fig. 5B). Subsequently, the selectivity was evaluated by adding 5 μL of 5 mM 4-NP and its analogs to the polyamide film. As depicted in Fig. 5C, only 4-NP induces a remarkable fluorescence quenching, however, other analogs show a negligible change. The results indicate that this portable polyamide film-based sensor has a significant potential for selective and visual detection of 4-NP in on-site and resource-constrained settings.

In conclusion, a novel pH-mediated reversible fluorescence nanoswitch has been successfully constructed for instant visual detection of 4-NP. This novel pH-mediated reversible fluorescence nanoswitch exhibits high sensitivity and excellent selectivity for 4-NP, especially it can selectively identify 4-NP from 2-NP and 3-NP. More significantly, this method has been applied to detect 4-NP in drinking and environmental water samples with satisfactory results. Furthermore, systematic studies demonstrate that the 4-NP-induced fluorescence quenching of PCDs derives from IFE. Compared to the fluorescence quenching induced by other mechanisms (e.g., ET and FRET), this approach avoids time-consuming analytical process, complex surface modification, and covalent linking between the fluorophore and quencher. Considering its simple, free-label, and quick responsiveness for 4-NP, a portable polyamide film-based sensor has been developed for visual detection of 4-NP. Such a simple and efficient sensing platform has a tremendous potential for the visual detection of 4-NP in on-site and resource-poor settings.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This study was financially supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (No. 21675131, 21273174) and the Natural Science Foundation of Chongqing (No. CSTC-2015jcyjB50001).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary material related to this article can be found, in the online version, at doi:https://doi.org/10.1016/j.jhazmat.2018.09.025.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: The following is Supplementary data to this article:

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Bioaugmentation of strain Methylobacterium sp. C1 towards p-nitrophenol removal with broad spectrum coaggregating bacteria in sequencing batch biofilm reactors
J. Hazard. Mater.
Catalytic reduction of 4-nitrophenol using gold nanoparticles biosynthesized by cell-free extracts of Aspergillus sp. WL-Au
J. Hazard. Mater.
Amperometric microbial biosensor for p-nitrophenol using Moraxella sp.-modified carbon paste electrode
